annotation.moveRationale: exf5 exchanges material, altering the pawn structure in the Latvian Gambit Accepted.
The Latvian gambit is an aggressive counterattack. It is utterly unsound but sharp and has some practical chances against unprepared players.
White develops a piece to a more active square, asserts control in the centre and over the d4 square, and attacks Black's e5-pawn. This is the most common opening played in chess.
e5 is the Open game (or the Double King's Pawn game). It is Black's classical response to 1.

Best by test. — Bobby Fischer

variations.name: Bilguer Variation

annotation.moveRationale: Be2 develops the bishop to a modest but solid square, preparing to castle in the Latvian Gambit Accepted.
Qe2 activates the queen, increasing pressure in the Latvian Gambit Accepted.
g6 prepares the fianchetto, aiming to place the bishop on a long diagonal in the Latvian Gambit Accepted.
Qh5+ delivers check, seizing the initiative in the Latvian Gambit Accepted.
Ne3 develops a piece to an active square in the Latvian Gambit Accepted.
fxe4 exchanges material, altering the pawn structure in the Latvian Gambit Accepted.
Nc4 develops a piece to an active square in the Latvian Gambit Accepted.
d6 advances a pawn, influencing the structure in the Latvian Gambit Accepted.
d4 impacts the center, shaping the pawn structure in the Latvian Gambit Accepted.
Black's queen has come lumbering out to poke the e5-knight in the ribs. Should White hold the line, or retreat.
White is threatening a fearsome queen check on h5, with the e5-knight ready to pounce on the g-pawn should Black advance it to g6. There's also the incidental threat of exf5.

variations.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 f5 3. Nxe5 Qf6 4. d4 d6 5. Nc4 fxe4 6. Qh5+ g6 7. Qe2

variations.name: Foltys Variation

annotation.moveRationale: d3 reinforces the center solidly, maintaining flexibility in the Latvian Gambit Accepted.
Black threatens to play. d5 with beautiful development and only a slightly misplaced queen to worry about.
Black remains a pawn down, but White's knight has run off, so Black may now dream of playing …d5 in one move. That pawn on e4 will have to be cleared out of the way first, with /4.
Black's queen has come lumbering out to poke the e5-knight in the ribs. Should White hold the line, or retreat.

variations.pgn: 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 f5 3. Nxe5 Qf6 4. Nc4 fxe4 5. d3

variations.name: Foltys-Leonhardt Variation

annotation.moveRationale: d3 reinforces the center solidly, maintaining flexibility in the Latvian Gambit Accepted.

White's c3-knight is threatening to capture on e4 hitting the queen on f6, and taking advantage of this diversion to retreat safely to c3. Were the queen not on f6, Black could turn the tables after Nxe4 with.
